title: Decorator basics

publication.changeSummary: Language-specific source-linked draft lesson for Decorator basics; independent technical, source, pedagogy, accessibility, and runnable-example review remains required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: The Python Language Reference; Python 3.14; Data Model iterator protocol plus yield expressions, generator functions, and decorators; atomic topic: Decorator basics
sources.claim: A function decorator receives a callable and returns a replacement callable, usually forwarding arbitrary arguments and preserving metadata with functools.wraps.

sources.locator: The Python Standard Library; Python 3.14; itertools, functools.wraps, and collections.abc iterator documentation; atomic topic: Decorator basics
sources.claim: A function decorator receives a callable and returns a replacement callable, usually forwarding arbitrary arguments and preserving metadata with functools.wraps.

canonicalExplanation.summary: A function decorator receives a callable and returns a replacement callable, usually forwarding arbitrary arguments and preserving metadata with functools.wraps.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A timing decorator uses @wraps, accepts *args and **kwargs, returns the wrapped result, and records only safe duration metadata. Your goal is to implement and explain a small function decorator with correct argument forwarding. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Core collections and text; Functions, scope and recursion; Scope and closures; Parameter kinds and argument binding. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Language-specific operational model
sections.body: A function decorator receives a callable and returns a replacement callable, usually forwarding arbitrary arguments and preserving metadata with functools.wraps. This model is applied through the declared Python: Beginner to Intermediate behavior rather than a language-neutral slogan.

sections.heading: Boundary and failure model
sections.body: Decoration normally occurs at definition/import time, wrapper signatures and descriptors matter, state can be shared across calls, async/generator functions need compatible wrappers, and order changes composition. The learner must reject this documented misconception: A decorator runs only when the function is called and functools.wraps automatically forwards arguments and results.

includes: Wrap callables while preserving metadata and making behavior changes explicit.
Implement and explain a small function decorator with correct argument forwarding.
excludes: Decoration normally occurs at definition/import time, wrapper signatures and descriptors matter, state can be shared across calls, async/generator functions need compatible wrappers, and order changes composition.

outcomes: Implement and explain a small function decorator with correct argument forwarding.

examples.title: Decorator basics: direct language case
examples.situation: A timing decorator uses @wraps, accepts *args and **kwargs, returns the wrapped result, and records only safe duration metadata.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Observe the concrete Python: Beginner to Intermediate state or tool result: A timing decorator uses @wraps, accepts *args and **kwargs, returns the wrapped result, and records only safe duration metadata.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.

Step 3 — Verify the result with this correction: Decoration replaces the callable at definition time; the wrapper must explicitly forward arguments, result, and failure behavior, while functools.wraps preserves metadata.
examples.result: The example meets the atomic outcome because Decoration replaces the callable at definition time; the wrapper must explicitly forward arguments, result, and failure behavior, while functools.wraps preserves metadata.

examples.title: Decorator basics: boundary and transfer case
examples.situation: Two decorators are applied in documented order, and a test proves exceptions and return values pass through unchanged.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Name what changed from the direct case: Two decorators are applied in documented order, and a test proves exceptions and return values pass through unchanged.

Step 2 — Keep the language boundary explicit: Decoration normally occurs at definition/import time, wrapper signatures and descriptors matter, state can be shared across calls, async/generator functions need compatible wrappers, and order changes composition.
Step 3 — Reject the tempting but incorrect rule: A decorator runs only when the function is called and functools.wraps automatically forwards arguments and results.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Implement and explain a small function decorator with correct argument forwarding.

misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Predict both examples and identify the first observable contradiction in this belief: A decorator runs only when the function is called and functools.wraps automatically forwards arguments and results.

analogy.example: a one-pass production route that can build a bounded result, expose an iterator, pause lazily, or wrap a callable can introduce Decorator basics, after the canonical Python: Beginner to Intermediate rule is stated first.
limitations: StopIteration, suspension, close behavior, metadata, wrapper order, and memory allocation require exact Python semantics
